rationale: The citing paper uses John et al. (2017) in its theory development to support a key premise: social-media likes/comments can be a poor proxy for true brand engagement. This is not just a background list citation; it is invoked at the exact point where the authors motivate their core distinction between sender engagement and brand engagement. That distinction is central to the paper's hypotheses and interpretation of results across social media, ads, and experiments.
relation_description: The citing paper treats John et al. (2017) as prior empirical grounding that surface social actions (e.g., likes) may not translate into meaningful brand outcomes. It then builds its own framework and tests around this premise by contrasting sender reactions with brand-oriented outcomes.
